author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew XIX. 16.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And, behold, one came and said unto Him, Good Master, by doing what, shall I inherit eternal life?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Some indeed accuse this young man, as one dissembling and ill-minded, and coming with a temptation to Jesus, but I, though I would not say he was not fond of money, and under subjection to his wealth, since Christ in fact convicted him of being such a character, yet a dissembler I would by no means call him, both because it is not safe to venture on things uncertain, and especially in blame, and because Mark hath taken away this suspicion; for he saith, that “having come running unto Him, and kneeling to Him, he besought Him,” and that “Jesus beheld him, and loved him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But great is the tyranny of wealth, and it is manifest hence; I mean, that though we be virtuous as to the rest, this ruins all besides.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: With reason hath Paul also affirmed it to be the root of all evils in general.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For the love of money is the root of all evils,”he saith.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore then doth Christ thus reply to him, saying, “There is none good?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because He came unto Him as a mere man, and one of the common sort, and a Jewish teacher; for this cause then as a man He discourses with him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And indeed in many instances He replies to the secret thoughts of them that come unto Him; as when He saith, “We worship we know what;”and, “If I bear witness of myself, my witness is not true.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When therefore He saith, “There is none good;” not as putting Himself out from being good doth He say this, far from it; for he said not, “Why dost thou call me good?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I am not good;” but, “there is none good,” that is, none amongst men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And when He saith this self-same thing, He saith it not as depriving even men of goodness, but in contradistinction to the goodness of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also He added, “But one, that is, God;” and He said not, “but my Father,” that thou mightest learn that He had not revealed Himself to the young man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So also further back He called men evil, saying, “If ye, being evil, know how to give good gifts to your children.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed there too He called them evil, not as condemning the whole race as evil (for by “ye,” He means not “ye men”), but comparing the goodness that is in men with the goodness of God, He thus named it; therefore also He added, “How much more shall your Father give good things to them that ask Him?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what was there to urge Him,or what the profit that He should answer in this way?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He leads him on by little and little, and teaches him to be far from all flattery, drawing him off from the things upon each, and fastening him upon God, and persuading him to seek after the things to come, and to know that which is really good, and the root and fountain of all things, and to refer the honors to Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since also when He saith, “Call no one master upon earth,” it is in contradistinction to Himself He saith this, and that they might learn what is the chief sovereignty over all things that are.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither was it a small forwardness the young man had shown up to this time in having fallen into such a desire; and when of the rest some were tempting, some were coming to Him for the cure of diseases, either their own or others, he for eternal life was both coming to Him, and discoursing with Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For fertile was the land and rich, but the multitude of the thorns choked the seed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Mark at any rate how he is prepared thus far for obedience to the commandments.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “By doing what,” he saith, “shall I inherit eternal life?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So ready was he for the performance of the things that should be told him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if he had come unto Him, tempting Him, the evangelist would have declared this also to us, as He doth also with regard to the others, as in the case of the lawyer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And though himself had been silent, Christ could not have suffered him to lie concealed, but would have convicted him plainly, or at least would have intimated it, so that he should not seem to have deceived Him, and to be hidden, and thereby have suffered hurt.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If he had come unto Him tempting, he would not have departed sorrowing for what he heard.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This was not at any rate ever the feeling of any of the Pharisees, but they grew fierce when their mouths were stopped.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But not so this man; but he goeth away cast down, which is no little sign that not with an evil will he had come unto Him, but with one too feeble, and that he did indeed desire life, but was held in subjection by another and most grievous feeling.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore when Christ said, “If thou wilt enter into life, keep the commandments,” he saith, “Which?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not tempting, far from it, but supposing there were some others besides those of the law that should procure him life, which was like one who was very desirous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then since Jesus mentioned those out of the law, he saith, “All these things have I kept from my youth up.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And neither at this did he stop, but again asks, “What lack I yet?” which itself again was a sign of his very earnest desire.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith Christ?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since He was going to enjoin something great, He setteth forth the recompenses, and saith, “If thou wilt be perfect, go and sell that thou hast, and give to the poor, and thou shalt have treasure in Heaven: and come, and follow me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how many prizes, how many crowns, He appoints for this race?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If he had been tempting, He would not have told him these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But now He both saith it, and in order to draw him on, He also shows him the reward to be great, and leaves it all to his own will, by all means throwing into the shade that which seemed to be grievous in His advice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore even before mentioning the conflicts and the toil, He shows him the prize, saying “If thou wilt be perfect,” and then saith, “Sell that thou hast, and give to the poor,” and straightway again the rewards, “Thou shalt have treasure in Heaven; and come, and follow me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed to follow Him is a great recompense.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And thou shalt have treasure in Heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For since his discourse was of money, even of all did He advise him to strip himself, showing that he loses not what he hath, but adds to his possessions, He gave him more than He required him to give up; and not only more, but also as much greater as Heaven is greater than earth, and yet more so.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But He called it a treasure, showing the plenteousness of the recompense, its permanency, its security, so far as it was possible by human similitudes to intimate it to the hearer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is not then enough to despise wealth, but we must also maintain poor men, and above all things follow Christ; that is, do all the things that are ordered by Him, be ready for slaughter and daily death.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For if any man will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross, and follow me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that to cast away one’s money is a much less thing than this last commandment, to shed even one’s very blood; yet not a little doth our being freed from wealth contribute towards this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But when the young man heard it, he went away sorrowful.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: After this the evangelist, as it were to show that he hath not felt anything it was unlikely he should feel, saith, “For he hadgreat possessions.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they that have little are not equally held in subjection, as they that are overflowed with great affluence, for then the love of it becomes more tyrannical.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Which thing I cease not always saying, that the increase of acquisitions kindles the flame more, and renders the getters poorer, inasmuch as it puts them in greater desire, and makes them have more feeling of their want.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See, for example, even here what strength did this passion exhibit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Him that had come to Him with joy and forwardness, when Christ commanded him to cast away his riches, it so overwhelmed and weighed down, as not to suffer him so much as to answer touching these things, but silenced and become dejected and sullen to go away.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith Christ?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “How hardly shall the rich enter into the kingdom of Heaven!”blaming not riches but them that are held in subjection by them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if the rich man “hardly,” much more the covetous man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if not to give one’s own be an hindrance to entering the kingdom, even to take of other men’s goods, think how much fire it heapeth up.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why can it have been, however, that He said to His disciples, that “hardly shall a rich man enter in,” they being poor men, and having no possessions?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Instructing them not to be ashamed of their poverty, and, as it were, excusing Himself to them for suffering them to have nothing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But having said it was hard; as He proceeds, He shows that it is even impossible, and not merely impossible, but even in the highest degree impossible; and this He showed by the comparison concerning the camel and theneedle.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “It is easier” saith He, “for a camel to enter in by the eye of a needle,than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of Heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence it is shown, that there is no ordinary reward for them that are rich, and are able to practise self command.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also He affirmed it to be a work of God, that He might show that great grace is needed for him who is to achieve this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: At least, when the disciples were troubled, He said, “With men this is impossible; but with God all things are possible.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And wherefore are the disciples troubled, being poor, yea, exceedingly poor?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore then are they confounded?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Being in pain about the salvation of the rest, and having a great affection for all, and having already taken upon themselves the tender bowels of teachers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They were at least in such trembling and fear for the whole world from this declaration, as to need much comfort.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore, having first “beheld them, He said unto them, The things which are impossible with men, are possible with God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For with a mild and meek look, having soothed their shuddering mind, and having put an end to their distress (for this the evangelist signified by saying, “He beheld them”), then by His words also He relieves them, bringing before them God’s power, and so making them feel confidence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if thou wilt learn the manner of it likewise, and how what is impossible may become possible, hear.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Born either for this end did He say, “The things which are impossible with men, are possible with God,” that thou shouldest give it up, and abstain, as from things impossible; but that having considered the greatness of the good work, thou shouldest hasten to it readily, and having besought God to assist thee in these noble contests, shouldest attain unto life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then should this become possible?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If thou cast away what thou hast, if thou empty thyself of thy wealth, if thou refrain from the wicked desire.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in proof that He does not refer it to God alone, but that to this end He said it, that thou shouldest know the vastness of the good work, hear what follows.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when Peter had said, “Behold, we have forsaken all, and followed Thee,” and had asked, “What shall we have therefore?” having appointed the reward for them; He added, “And every one who hath forsaken houses, or lands, or brothers, or sisters, or father, or mother, shall receive an hundred fold, and shall inherit eternal life.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus that which is impossible becometh possible.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But how may this very thing be done, one may say, to forsake these? how is it possible for him that is once sunk in such lust of wealth, to recover himself?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If he begin to empty himself of his possessions, and cut off what are superfluous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so shall he both advance further, and shall run on his course more easily afterwards.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do not then seek all at once, but gently, and by little and little, ascend this ladder, that leads thee up to Heaven.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For like as those in fevers having acrid bile abounding within them, when they cast in thereon meats and drinks, so far from quenching their thirst, do even kindle the flame; so also the covetous, when they cast in their wealth upon this wicked lust more acrid than that bile, do rather inflame it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For nothing so stays it as to refrain for a time from the lust of gain, like as acrid bile is stayed by abstinence and evacuations.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But this itself, by what means will it be done? one may say.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If thou consider, that whilst rich, thou wilt never cease thirsting, and pining with the lust of more; but being freed from thy possessions, thou wilt be able also to stay this disease.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do not then encompass thyself with more, lest thou follow after things unattainable, and be incurable, and be more miserable than all, being thus frantic.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For answer me, whom shall we affirm to be tormented and pained? him that longs after costly meats and drinks, and is not able to enjoy them as he will, or him that hath not such a desire?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is quite clear one must say, him that desires, but cannot obtain what he desires.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this is so painful, to desire and not to enjoy, to thirst and not to drink, that Christ desiring to describe hell to us, described it in this way, and introduced the rich man thus tormented.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For longing for a drop of water, and not enjoying it, this was his punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So then he that despises wealth quiets the desire, but he that desires to be richhath inflamed it more, and not yet doth he stay; but though he have got ten thousand talents, he desireth as much more; though he obtain these, again he aims at twice as much more, and going on he desires even the mountains, and the earth, and the sea, and all to become gold for him, being mad with a kind of new and fearful madness, and one that can never thus be extinguished.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And that thou mightest learn, that not by addition but by taking away this evil is stayed; if thou hadst ever had an absurd desire to fly and to be borne through the air, how wouldest thou extinguish this unreasonable desire?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By fashioning wings, and preparing other instruments, or by convincing the mind that it is desiring things impossible, and that one should attempt none of these things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is quite plain, that by convincing the mind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But that, thou mayest say, is impossible.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But this again is more impossible, to find a limit for this desire.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed it is more easy for men to fly, than to make this lust cease by an addition of more.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when the objects of desire are possible, one may be soothed by the enjoyment of them, but when they are impossible, one must labor for one thing, to draw ourselves off from the desire, as otherwise at least it is not possible to recover the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore that we may not have superfluous sorrows, let us forsake the love of money that is ever paining, and never endures to hold its peace, and let us remove ourselves to another love, which both makes us happy, and hath great facility, and let us long after the treasures above.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither is the labor here so great, and the gain is unspeakable, and it is not possible for him to fail of them who is but in any wise watchful and sober, and despises the things present; even as on the other hand, as to him that is a slave to these last, and is utterly given up to them, it as altogether of necessity that he fail of those better riches.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Considering then all these things, put away the wicked desire of wealth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither couldest thou say this, that it gives the things present, though it deprive us of the things to come, albeit even if this were so, this were extreme punishment, and vengeance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But now not even this may be.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For besides hell, and before that hell, even here it casts thee into a more grievous punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For many houses hath this lust overthrown, and fierce wars hath it stirred up, and compelled men to end their lives by a violent death; and before these dangers it ruins the nobleness of the soul, and is wont often to make him that hath it cowardly, and unmanly, and rash, and false, and calumnious, and ravenous, and over-reaching, and all the worst things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But seeing perhaps the brightness of the silver, and the multitude of the servants, and the beauty of the buildings, the court paid in the market-place, art thou bewitched thereby?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What remedy then may there be for this evil wound?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If thou consider how these things affect thy soul, how dark, and desolate, and foul they render it, and how ugly; if thou reckon with how many evils these things were acquired, with how many labors they are kept, with how many dangers: or rather they are not kept unto the end, but when thou hast escaped the attempts of all, death coming on thee is often wont to remove these things into the hand of thine enemies, and goeth and taketh thee with him destitute, drawing after thee none of these things, save the wounds and the sores only, which the soul received from these, before its departing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When then thou seest any one resplendent outwardly with raiment and large attendance, lay open his conscience, and thou shalt see many a cobweb within, and much dust.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Consider Paul, Peter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Consider John, Elias, or rather the Son of God Himself, who hath not where to lay His head.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Be an imitator of Him, and of His servants, and imagine to thyself the unspeakable riches of these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if having obtained a little sight by these, thou shouldest be darkened again, as in any shipwreck when a storm hath come on, hear the declaration of Christ, which affirms, that it is impossible “for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of Heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And against this declaration set the mountains, and the earth, and the sea; and all things, if thou wilt, supposeto be gold; for thou shalt see nothing equal to the loss arising to thee from thence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And thou indeed makest mention of acres of land, so many and so many, and of houses ten or twenty or even more, and of baths as many, and of slaves a thousand, or twice as many, and of chariots fastened with silver and overlaid with gold; but I say this, that if each one of you that are rich were to leave this poverty (for these things are poverty compared with what I am about to say), and were possessed of a whole world, and each of them had as many men as are now everywhere on land and sea, and each a world both sea and land, and everywhere buildings, and cities, and nations, and from every side instead of water, instead of fountains, gold flowed up for him, I would not say those who are thus rich are worth three farthings, when they are cast out of the kingdom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if now aiming at riches that perish, when they miss them, they are tormented, if they should obtain a perception of those unspeakable blessings, what then will suffice for consolation for them?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: There is nothing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Tell me not then of the abundance of their possessions, but consider how great loss the lovers of this abundance undergo in consequence thereof, for these things losing Heaven, and being in the same state, as if any one after being cast out of the highest honor in kings’ courts, having a dung heap, were to pride himself on that.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the storing up of money differs nothing from that, or rather that is even the better.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that is serviceable both for husbandry, and for heating a bath, and for other such uses, but the buried gold for none of these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And would it were merely useless; but as it is, it kindles moreover many furnaces for him that hath it, unless he use it rightly; countess evils at least spring therefrom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore they that are without used to call the love of money the citadelof evils; but the blessed Paul spake much better and more vividly, pronouncing it “the root of all evils.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Considering then all these things, let us emulate the things worthy of emulation, not gorgeous buildings not costly estates, but the men that have much confidence towards God, those that have riches in Heaven, the owners of those treasures, them that are really rich, them that are poor for Christ’s sake, that we may attain unto the good things of eternity by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, with whom be unto the Father, together with the Holy Ghost, glory, might, honor, now and always and world without end.
